volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Braggart Soldier (Section 2.1.110 - Section 2.2.235)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Miles Gloriosus, or The Braggart Captain

sections.label: Section 2.1.110
sections.original: sublinit os illi lenae , matri mulieris ,
sections.translation: he tricked this procuress , the mother of the damsel , whom my master loved . For , unknown to her mother , he put the daughter on board ship , and carried this woman , against her will , hither to Ephesus . Soon as I knew that the lady of my master was carried off from Athens ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.115
sections.original: ego quantum vivos possum mihi navem paro ,
sections.translation: as quickly as ever I was able , I procured for myself a ship : I embarked , that I might carry tidings of this matter to my master at Naupactum . When we had got out to sea , some pirates , as they had hoped to do , took that ship on board of which I was ; thus I was undone before I reached my master , for whom I had commenced to proceed on my voyage .

sections.label: Section 2.1.120
sections.original: ille quí me cepit dat me huic dono militi .
sections.translation: He that took me , gave me as a present to this same Captain . After he had taken me home to his own house , I saw there that favorite of my master who lived at Athens . When , on the other hand , she perceived me , she gave me a sign with her eyes not to address her by name . Afterwards , when there was an opportunity ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.125
sections.original: conqueritur mecum mulier fortunas suas :
sections.translation: the damsel complained to me of her hard fate . She said that she wished to escape to Athens from this house , that she was attached to him , that master of mine who lived at Athens , and that she had never hated any one more thoroughly than this same Captain . As I discovered the feelings of the damsel ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.130
sections.original: cepi tabellas , consignavi , clanculum
sections.translation: I took tablets , sealed them in private , and gave them to a certain merchant to carry to him ( my master , I mean , who was at Athens , and who had so loved her ), in order that he might come hither . He did not slight the message , for he both is come , and is lodging here next door ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.135
sections.original: apud suom paternum hóspitem , lepidum senem ;
sections.translation: with his host , a friend of his father ’ s , a nice old man . He , too , gives every assistance to his guest in his amour , and encourages and seconds us with his help and his advice . Therefore , here ( pointing to the CAPTAIN ’ S house ) , in-doors , I have found a grand contrivance , by which to cause these lovers , each , to meet the other .

sections.label: Section 2.1.140
sections.original: nam unum conclave , concubinae quod dedit
sections.translation: For one room , which the Captain gave to his mistress for no one but herself to set foot in , in that same room I have dug a hole through the party-wall , in order that there may secretly be an ingress for the damsel from the one house to the other . And this I have done with the knowledge of the old gentleman ; ’ twas he that gave the advice .

sections.label: Section 2.1.145
sections.original: nam meus conservos est homo haud magni preti ,
sections.translation: But my fellow-servant , whom the Captain has given as a keeper to his mistress , is a person of no great worth . By clever contrivances and ingenious devices , we will throw dust [*] in his eyes , and we will make him so as not to see what he really does see .

sections.label: Section 2.1.150
sections.original: et mox ne erretis , haec duarum hodie vicem
sections.translation: And that you may not hereafter make mistakes , this damsel to-day , in this house and in that , will perform in turn a double part , and will be the same , but will pretend to be another , person . Thus will the keeper of the damsel be gulled . But there is a noise [*] at the door here of the old gentleman , our neighbour .

sections.label: Section 2.1.155
sections.original: ipse exit : hic illest lepidus quem dixi senex .
sections.translation: ’ Tis himself coming out ’ tis he , the nice old man that I was speaking of .

sections.label: Section 2.2.156
sections.original: Ni hércle diffregéritis talos pósthac quemque in tégulis
sections.translation: Faith , if you don ’ t in future smash his ankle-bones for any stranger that you see on my tiles , I will cut you so with lashes as to make thongs of your sides . My neighbours , i ’ faith , are overlookers of what is going on in my own house ; so often are they peeping down through the skylight [*] . And now , therefore , I give you all notice ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.160
sections.original: quemque a milite hoc videritis hominem in nostris tegulis ,
sections.translation: whatever person of this Captain ’ s household you shall see upon our tiles , except Palaestrio only , push him headlong here into the street . Suppose he says that he is following some hen , or pigeon , or monkey ; woe be to you , if you don ’ t badly maul the fellow even to death . And so , that they may commit no infringement against the laws of dice [*] ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.165
sections.original: adcuratote ut sine talis domi agitent convivium .
sections.translation: do you take good care that they keep holiday at home without any ankle-bones at all .

sections.label: Section 2.2.166
sections.original: Nescio quid malefactum a nostra hic familiast , quantum audio :
sections.translation: Something amiss ,— what , I know not , has been done him by our family so far as I can hear , inasmuch as the old man has ordered the ankles of my fellow-servants to be broken . But he has excepted me ; nothing care I what he does to the rest of them . I ’ ll accost the old man . ( Advances .)

sections.label: Section 2.2.169b
sections.original: Estne advorsum hic qui advenit Palaestrio ?
sections.translation: The person that is coming this way , is he coming towards me ? He comes as if he was coming to me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.170
sections.original: Quid agis , Periplectomene ?
sections.translation: How do you do , Periplecomenus ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.170b
sections.original: Hau multos homines , si optandum foret ,
sections.translation: There are not many men , if I were to wish , whom I would rather now see and meet with than yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.2.171b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ? What disturbance have you with our family ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.172b
sections.original: Occisi sumus .
sections.translation: We are done for .

sections.label: Section 2.2.173
sections.original: Quid negotist ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.173b
sections.original: Res palamst .
sections.translation: The thing ’ s discovered .

sections.label: Section 2.2.173c
sections.original: Quae res palamst ?
sections.translation: What thing ’ s discovered ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.173d
sections.original: De tegulis
sections.translation: Some one just now of your household was looking in from the tiles

sections.label: Section 2.2.175
sections.original: per nostrum impluvium intus ápud nos Philocomasium atque hospitem
sections.translation: through our skylight at Philocomasium and my guest as they were toying together .

sections.label: Section 2.2.176b
sections.original: Quis homo id vidit ?
sections.translation: What person saw it ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.176c
sections.original: Tuos conservos .
sections.translation: Your fellow-servant .

sections.label: Section 2.2.176d
sections.original: Quis is homost ?
sections.translation: Which person was it ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.177
sections.original: Nescio , ita abripuit repente sese subito .
sections.translation: I don ’ t know ; he took himself off so suddenly — in an instant .

sections.label: Section 2.2.177b
sections.original: Suspicor
sections.translation: I suspect I ’ m ruined .

sections.label: Section 2.2.178b
sections.original: Vbi abit , conclamo : heus quid agis tu inquam in tegulis ?
sections.translation: When he went away , I cried : Hallo ! you sir ! said I , what are you doing upon the tiles ? As he went away he replied to me in these terms , that he was following a stray ’ d monkey .

sections.label: Section 2.2.180
sections.original: Vae mihi misero , quoi pereundumst propter nihili bestiam .
sections.translation: Woe to wretched me ! that I must be ruined for a worthless beast . But is Philocomasium there with you even still ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.181b
sections.original: Quom exibam , hic erat .
sections.translation: When I came out , she was there .

sections.label: Section 2.2.182
sections.original: I sis , iube transire huc quantum possit , se ut videant domi
sections.translation: If she is , then bid her return to our house as soon as ever she can , that the servants may see that she is at home ; unless , indeed , she wishes that we , who are slaves , her fellow-slaves [*] , should all be given up together to tortures by the cross on account of her courting .

sections.label: Section 2.2.185
sections.original: Dixi ego istuc ; nisi quid aliud vis .
sections.translation: I bade her do so ; unless you would aught else .

sections.label: Section 2.2.185b
sections.original: Volo . hoc ei dicito :
sections.translation: I would . Tell her this : that , by my troth , she must not hesitate at all to bring in play her skill and cleverness .

sections.label: Section 2.2.186b
sections.original: Quem ad modum ?
sections.translation: In what way ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.187
sections.original: Vt eum , qui hic se vidit , verbis vincat , ne is se viderit .
sections.translation: That by her words she may persuade him who saw her here at your house , that he did not see her .

sections.label: Section 2.2.188
sections.original: siquidem centiens hic visa sit , tamen infitias eat .
sections.translation: Even though she were seen a hundred times over , still let her deny it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.189
sections.original: os habet , linguam , perfidiam , malitiam atque audaciam ,
sections.translation: at home she has impudence [*] , a lying tongue , perfidiousness , malice , and boldness , self-conceit , assurance , and deceitfulness ,—

sections.label: Section 2.2.190
sections.original: qui arguat se , eum contra vincat iureiurando suo :
sections.translation: Should he accuse her , on the other hand let her convince him with her oath .

sections.label: Section 2.2.191
sections.original: domi habet animum falsiloquom , falsificum , falsiiurium ,
sections.translation: at home she has wiles ,— at home captivating contrivances ,— stratagems at home .

sections.label: Section 2.2.193
sections.original: nam mulier holitori numquam supplicat , si quast mala :
sections.translation: ( Aside .) For , if she is at all inclined to ill , a woman never goes begging [*] to the gardener for material , she has a garden at home and a stock of her own for all mischievous contrivances ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.195
sections.original: Ego istaec , si erit hic , nuntiabo . sed quid est , Palaestrio ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell her this , if she shall be in-doors here ( pointing to his house .) But what is it , Palaestrio , that you are considering with yourself in your mind ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.196b
sections.original: Paulisper tace ,
sections.translation: Be silent a moment , while I am calling a council in my mind , and while I am considering what I am to do , what plan I must contrive , on the other hand , as a match for my crafty fellow-servant , who has seen her billing here in your house ; so that what was seen may not have been seen .

sections.label: Section 2.2.200
sections.original: Quaere : ego hinc abscessero aps te huc interim . illuc sis vide ,
sections.translation: Do contrive one ; in the meantime , I ’ ll retire hence to a distance from you , to this spot . ( He retires to a distance .) Look at him , please ( to the AUDIENCE ) , revolving his cares with brow severe , how he stands . He strikes his breast with his fingers I fancy he ’ s about to call his heart outside . See , he shifts his posture ; again he places his left hand upon his left thigh . His right hand is reckoning down his plans upon his fingers ; in despair he strikes his thigh .

sections.label: Section 2.2.205
sections.original: dexterum . ita vehementer icit : quod agat aegre suppetit .
sections.translation: His right hand is moving rapidly [*] ; with difficulty does it suggest what he is to do . He snaps his fingers now ; he ’ s striving hard ; full oft he changes his position . But see how he shakes his head ; it pleases him not what he has hit upon . Whatever it is , nothing crude will he bring forth , something well-digested will he produce . But see , he is building ; he has placed his hand as a pillar [*] beneath his chin .

sections.label: Section 2.2.210
sections.original: apage , non placet profecto mi illaec aedificatio ;
sections.translation: Have done with it in truth , this mode of building pleases me not ; for I have heard say that the head of a foreign Poet [*] is wont to be supported thus , over whom two guards are ever at all hours keeping watch . Bravo ! how becomingly he stands ,— i ’ faith , how like a very slave [*] , and how faithful to his part . Never , this day , will he rest , before he has completed that which he is in search of .

sections.label: Section 2.2.215
sections.original: habet opinor . age si quid agis , vigila , ne somno stude ,
sections.translation: He has it , I suspect . Come — to the business you ’ re about : keep wide awake , think not of sleep ; unless , indeed , you wish to be keeping your watch here all checquered o ’ er with stripes . ’ Tis I , that am talking to you ; schemer , don ’ t you know that I am speaking to you ? Palaestrio ! awake , say ; arouse yourself , I say ; ’ tis daylight now , I say .

sections.label: Section 2.2.218b
sections.original: Audio .
sections.translation: I hear you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.219
sections.original: Viden hostis tibi adesse tuoque tergo obsidium ? consule ,
sections.translation: Don ’ t you see that the enemy is upon you , and that siege is being laid to your back ? Take counsel , then ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.220
sections.original: arripe opem auxiliumque ad hanc rem : propere hoc , non placide decet .
sections.translation: obtain aid and assistance in this matter ; the hastily , not the leisurely , is befitting here . Get the start of them in some way , and in some direction this moment lead around your troops . Close round the enemy in siege ; prepare the convoy for our side . Cut off the enemy ’ s provision , secure yourself a passage , by which supplies and provision may be enabled in safety to reach yourself and your forces . Look to this business ; the emergency is sudden .

sections.label: Section 2.2.226
sections.original: reperi , comminiscere , cedo calidum consilium cito ,
sections.translation: Invent — contrive — this instant give us some clever plan ; so that that which has been seen here within , may not have been seen ; that which has been done , may not have been done . There , my man , you undertake a great enterprise ; lofty the defences which you erect . If you yourself alone but say you undertake this , I have a certainty

sections.label: Section 2.2.230
sections.original: nos inimicos profligare posse .
sections.translation: that we are able to rout our foes .

sections.label: Section 2.2.230b
sections.original: Dico et recipio
sections.translation: I do say so , and I do undertake it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.231b
sections.original: Et ego impetrare dico id quod petis .
sections.translation: And I do pronounce that you shall obtain that which you desire .

sections.label: Section 2.2.231c
sections.original: At te Iuppiter
sections.translation: May Jupiter kindly bless you then !

sections.label: Section 2.2.232b
sections.original: Auden participare me quod commentus escommentu's ?
sections.translation: But , friend , do you impart to me the plan which you have devised .

sections.label: Section 2.2.232c
sections.original: Tace ,
sections.translation: Be silent , then , while I am inducting you in the direction of my devices ; that you may know as well as my own self my plans .

sections.label: Section 2.2.234b
sections.original: Salva sumes indidem .
sections.translation: The same you shall receive safe from the same spot where you have deposited them .

sections.label: Section 2.2.235
sections.original: Erus meus elephanti corio circumtentust , non suo ,
sections.translation: My master is surrounded with the hide of an elephant , not his own , and has no more wisdom than a stone .
